OIIX A4458/24.explanation: This is an important message for pilots flying between Tehran and Kabul airspaces. If you're flying from Kabul into Tehran, you must contact Tehran air traffic control at least 5 minutes before crossing the border, using specific radio frequencies for your route. However, if you're flying from Tehran into Kabul, you do so at your own risk. Air traffic services will only be provided up to the border with Kabul airspace, meaning you're on your own once you enter Kabul's airspace.

OIIX A4459/24.explanation: This is a warning from Iran to its airlines about flying in, out of, or over Afghanistan. It says that the airspace over Afghanistan is dangerous because there aren't proper air traffic control services, there's a risk from militant groups, and it's hard to deal with emergencies. Iranian airlines are told to be very careful and consider these serious risks when planning any flights in that area.

OIIX A4460/24.explanation: This is an official warning from Iran's aviation authority to Iranian airlines and pilots. It tells them there's a serious security risk when flying over certain areas in and around Ukraine, Rostov (Russia), and Moldova. The danger comes from increased military activity, anti-aircraft weapons, and the potential for mistakes or misunderstandings due to the ongoing conflict in Ukraine. Pilots are advised to carefully consider these risks when planning flights in these regions.

OIIX A4461/24.explanation: This is an urgent safety warning for all aircraft operators. They are strongly advised not to fly into the airspace over Sudan (known as Khartoum FIR) because it is currently very dangerous. There's a high risk from anti-aircraft weapons and intense military activity. This warning is issued by Iran's aviation authority and will be in effect from November 2024 until February 2025. Pilots should contact the Iran Civil Aviation Authority for more details and check the provided ICAO website for alternative flight routes.

OIIX A0712/26.explanation: An important air route, called N39, in Tehran's airspace (between DEMBA and OBRIX) will be closed for all planes flying through that area. This closure will last from February 28 to May 29, 2026. Planes flying west will need to use an alternative route through RADAL, IMKER, and ULDUS, flying no lower than 27,500 feet. Planes flying east will need to use an alternative route through ULDUS, ALKUP, IMLIM, OXADU, and OBRIX, flying no lower than 28,500 feet.

OIIX A0713/26.explanation: An air highway (called G208/L125) in the airspace over Tehran, Iran, is temporarily closed for planes that are just passing through the area. If a pilot planned to use this specific route, they now need to fly a different, specified path (via ROVAD direct to IKA). However, planes arriving at Tehran's main airports (Imam Khomeini, Mehrabad, and Payam) can still use a specific entry point (RADAL). This change is in effect from February 28 to May 10, 2026.

OIIX A0724/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in the Tehran region. It provides new, additional phone numbers (a direct line and a mobile number) to contact Air Traffic Control.

OIIX A0781/26.explanation: This is an important message for foreign airlines and aircraft. If you are flying into, out of, or through Iranian airspace (Tehran FIR), you must send your flight plans and any updates to a specific address: OIIXIFPS. You also need to follow the detailed procedures mentioned in Iran's aviation manual (AIP ENR 1.11). This rule is in effect from April 20, 2026, to July 19, 2026.

OIIX A0782/26.explanation: This is an important message for pilots flying in the Tehran airspace. Several specific high-frequency radio channels that pilots use to talk to Tehran air traffic information services are currently not working. This affects all types of flights, from the ground up to any altitude, and will last from April 20, 2026, until July 19, 2026. Pilots need to use alternative communication methods or frequencies during this time.

OIIX A0784/26.explanation: This NOTAM announces a new, permanent controlled airspace established south of Tehran, Iran. This new area starts at 19,500 feet and extends upwards indefinitely. At 20,000 feet, it's designated as Class D airspace, meaning pilots must communicate with air traffic control. Above 20,000 feet, it becomes Class A airspace, which is highly controlled and primarily for instrument flights. Tehran Air Traffic Control will manage this area 24 hours a day on frequency 133.400 MHz, using English and Persian. This change is important for pilots flying through this region.

OIIX A0785/26.explanation: This notice tells pilots that the radio frequencies they use to talk to air traffic control in various parts of Iran's airspace are changing. From April 20, 2026, until July 19, 2026, several existing frequencies for different sectors will be replaced by a new frequency, 258.400 MHz. Pilots flying in this area must use the new frequency for communication.

OIIX B0187/26.explanation: This is an update to air traffic rules for domestic flights in the Tehran airspace. It specifies new, mandatory routes that planes must follow when flying between two particular airports (OIII and OIMD) in Iran. These changes are in effect from April 20, 2026, to July 19, 2026.

OIIX A0793/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying within Iran. It changes the specific routes (called SIDs and STARs) that certain domestic flights must follow when traveling between particular airports, like from OIBK to OIMM, or from OISS to OICC, OICK, and OICI. These changes are part of a larger plan to manage air traffic and are detailed in an official aviation document.

OIIX A0863/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots about specific, mandatory flight paths that planes must use when flying to Tehran's Imam Khomeini International Airport (OIMM) from three other domestic airports (OISS, OIBB, and OIYY). It's part of a traffic management plan to ensure planes follow designated routes.

OIIX A0893/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots about several 'highways in the sky' (airways) in Iran that will be temporarily closed. These specific routes will be unavailable for aircraft to fly on from May 5, 2026, until August 3, 2026. Pilots will need to plan alternative routes during this time.

OIIX A0912/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in Iran. A specific air route, called Airway A647, between two points named NAZAR and RAGET, has been changed. Previously, aircraft might have only been able to fly in one direction on this segment, but now they can fly in both directions. This is a change to the air traffic rules for that specific 'highway in the sky'.

OIIX A0957/26.explanation: A vital navigation beacon, called VOR ANK, which helps aircraft find their way, is currently not working. It operates on frequency 112.700 MHz and will be out of service from May 12, 2026, until August 10, 2026. Once repaired, it will need to be tested by an aircraft in flight to ensure it's working correctly before it can be used again.

OIIX A0916/26.explanation: An area in Iran, specifically designated as OID121, will be restricted for all aircraft traffic from May 13 to June 21, 2026. This restriction will be active daily between 03:30 AM and 07:30 PM UTC, covering altitudes from the ground up to 30,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX B0209/26.explanation: There will be gun firing in a specific area from May 13 to June 21, 2026, daily between 03:30 UTC and 18:30 UTC. This activity will take place from the ground up to 13,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this danger area.

OIIX A0917/26.explanation: There will be gun firing exercises in a specific area in Iran from May 13 to June 21, 2026. These exercises will happen daily between 03:30 and 19:30 UTC. The affected airspace is from the ground up to 20,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this military activity.

OIIX A0973/26.explanation: This is an update to the 'Traffic Orientation Scheme' for domestic flights within the Tehran airspace. It changes specific flight paths (routes for taking off and landing) between various airports in Iran. Think of it as updating the 'road map' for planes flying between these cities.

OIIX A0974/26.explanation: This is an official aviation notice announcing changes to the specific flight paths (routes) that aircraft must follow when flying into or out of major airports in Iran (Tehran, Shiraz, Mashhad) and when traveling to or from neighboring airspaces like Muscat (Oman) and Baghdad (Iraq). It updates the 'Traffic Orientation Scheme' for international arrivals and departures, essentially changing the designated 'roads in the sky' for these regions.

OIIX A0933/26.explanation: An area called OID125 in the Tehran region will be a restricted airspace daily from May 14 to June 21, 2026, between 4:30 AM and 6:30 PM UTC. Aircraft will not be allowed to fly through this area from the ground up to 18,000 feet during those times. Pilots need to check their maps for more details.

OIIX A1021/26.explanation: This is a warning for all flights. It states that a dangerous area, possibly a conflict zone, might expand into international airspace over the Persian Gulf and part of the Oman Sea. This is due to security concerns, and flights should be cautious in that region.

OIIX A1032/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in the Tehran airspace. It means that all established air routes can now be navigated using a specific type of satellite-based navigation system called RNAV 5, starting from the lowest safe flying altitude. This is a standard update to navigation procedures.

OIIX A1036/26.explanation: The main radar system used by air traffic controllers in Tehran (Mehrabad) is currently out of order. This means that pilots flying in certain parts of the airspace around Tehran might not receive radar services, making it harder for controllers to track and guide aircraft. This situation will last from May 25, 2026, until June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1038/26.explanation: The main radar system at Tehran's Imam Khomeini Airport is currently not working. This means that air traffic controllers might not have radar information for planes flying in certain areas (sectors 1, 2, and 7) of the airspace. As a result, radar services in these specific areas may not be available to pilots. This situation is expected to last from May 25, 2026, until June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1039/26.explanation: A radar system at Kushk is currently broken and will be out of service until late June. This means that air traffic controllers in certain parts of the Tehran airspace (specifically sectors 1, 2, and 7) might not have radar information for planes flying through those areas. Pilots and controllers will need to use alternative procedures to manage air traffic safely.

OIIX A1040/26.explanation: A radar system at Kish airport is currently broken. This means that air traffic controllers will not have radar information for planes flying long distances in the area. Additionally, radar services might not be available in certain parts of the airspace controlled by Tehran, specifically in Sector 4 and Sector 5. This could affect how air traffic controllers guide planes until late June.

OIIX A1041/26.explanation: A radar system in Esfahan, Iran, is currently broken. This means that air traffic controllers might not be able to see planes on their radar screens in certain parts of the airspace, specifically in sectors 2, 3, 6 West, and 7. This situation affects flights that are already in the air (en-route) and is expected to last from May 25 to June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1042/26.explanation: A main radar system (LAR MSSR) in the Tehran region is currently broken and will not be working from May 25 to June 29, 2026. This means air traffic controllers will not have radar information for planes flying on their routes, and radar services might be unavailable in a specific air traffic control area known as Sector 4 (using frequency 133.400 MHz).

OIIX A1043/26.explanation: The radar system in Shiraz is currently broken, which means air traffic controllers won't have radar information for planes flying in certain areas (specifically parts of Sector 3 and Sector 4) within the Tehran flight region. This could affect flights that rely on instruments for navigation. This problem is expected to last from May 25 to June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1044/26.explanation: A radar system in Ahwaz, Iran, is currently broken. This means that air traffic controllers might not have radar information for planes flying in certain parts of the Ahwaz airspace (specifically Sector 2 and Sector 3). Pilots flying through these areas will need to rely on other communication and navigation methods. This issue is expected to last from May 25 to June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1045/26.explanation: A radar system near Tabriz, Iran, is currently broken and will be out of service until late June 2026. This means that air traffic controllers might not be able to see planes on their radar screens in some parts of the airspace, particularly in a specific control sector. Pilots flying through these areas will need to be aware that radar services might not be available.

OIIX A1056/26.explanation: This is a permanent update for pilots flying in Iran. A specific area called 'OIR42 Uromiyeh' that was previously restricted for flights has now been completely removed. This means aircraft can now fly through this area without any special permissions or limitations. Pilots should update their navigation charts accordingly.

OIIX A1006/26.explanation: An area in Iran, identified as OID84, will be off-limits for all aircraft, including both commercial and private planes, from May 28 to July 22, 2026. This restriction will be in effect every day during specific hours (between 03:30 AM and 07:30 PM UTC) and will cover altitudes from the ground up to 13,000 feet above sea level. Pilots must refer to official aviation documents for more information about this newly activated restricted zone.

OIIX A1008/26.explanation: An area in Iran (called OID126) will be restricted for aircraft from May 28, 2026, to July 22, 2026. During this period, the restriction will be active daily between 03:30 AM and 04:30 PM UTC. No aircraft will be allowed to fly in this area from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX B0248/26.explanation: Attention all aircraft: There will be gun firing exercises taking place in a specific area. This will happen daily from May 28, 2026, to July 22, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM local time. The danger zone extends from the ground up to 15,000 feet above sea level. Aircraft should avoid this area during these times.

OIIX A1053/26.explanation: This is an aviation notice about a specific area called OID122 in the Tehran region. This area will be restricted, meaning aircraft are not allowed to fly there, from the ground up to 16,000 feet. This restriction will be active daily from 02:30 AM to 08:30 PM UTC, starting on May 30, 2026, and ending on July 22, 2026. Pilots need to be aware of this restricted zone.

OIIX A1054/26.explanation: Attention pilots! A specific area within the Tehran airspace, identified as OID123, is now restricted. This means aircraft are not allowed to fly there. This restriction is active daily from May 30th to July 22nd, between 02:30 AM and 08:30 PM UTC. The restricted zone extends from the ground up to 18,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX A1055/26.explanation: An area called OID128 in the Tehran region will be restricted for aircraft. This means planes cannot fly there. It will be active daily from May 30, 2026, to July 22, 2026, between 02:30 AM and 04:30 PM UTC, from the ground up to 15,000 feet. Pilots must check their routes to avoid this area.

OIIX B0251/26.explanation: This is a warning for pilots that gun firing will be taking place in a specific area within Iran. The firing will occur daily between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM UTC, from May 30 to July 22, 2026. The affected area extends from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level. Aircraft should be aware of this activity.

OIIX B0252/26.explanation: Attention pilots! There will be gun firing in a specific area within Iran's airspace. This activity will take place daily from May 30, 2026, until July 22, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM UTC each day. The affected airspace is from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level. Aircraft should avoid this area during these times.

OIIX A1058/26.explanation: Attention all pilots and air travelers! From May 30th to July 22nd, daily between 3:30 AM and 8:30 PM UTC, there will be gun firing exercises in a specific area within Iran. This activity will take place from the ground up to 20,000 feet in the air. Aircraft should be aware of this military activity and avoid the designated area during these times for safety.

OIIX B0255/26.explanation: Attention pilots and air travelers! There will be gun firing exercises taking place in a specific area near Mashhad, Iran. These exercises will occur daily between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM local time, starting from May 30, 2026, and continuing until July 22, 2026. The affected airspace is from the ground up to 11,000 feet above sea level. Please be aware of this activity if flying in the region.

OIIX B0254/26.explanation: An area of airspace called OID124 is now restricted for aircraft. This means planes cannot fly there without special permission. It will be active every day from May 30th to July 22nd, 2026, specifically between 4:30 AM and 8:30 PM Universal Coordinated Time (UTC). The restricted zone starts from the ground and goes up to 13,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should check their aviation charts for details about this area.

OIIX A1101/26.explanation: All civilian drone and remotely controlled aircraft flights are banned in the airspace over Tehran, Iran, from June 2, 2026, to August 25, 2026. The only exception is agricultural spraying drones, but they must fly below 165 feet and have special permission beforehand.

OIIX A1102/26.explanation: This is a warning from Iran's aviation authority about a dangerous situation over a large area of international waters, from sea level up to 10,000 feet, between June 2 and July 2, 2026. Due to increased military and naval activities, electronic warfare, and air defense readiness, there's a high risk of civil aircraft being mistaken for something else, sudden flight path changes, GPS and communication problems, more military aircraft intercepts, and quick closures of air routes. All aircraft operators, especially Iranian ones, are advised to be extremely careful, assess risks before flying, constantly check for updates, have backup plans for diversions or communication failures, and immediately follow air traffic control instructions.

OIIX A1112/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots and air traffic controllers in the Tehran region of Iran. It announces a temporary update to the established flight paths for international planes flying into and out of the area. Specifically, it changes certain routes (identified by names like PAXAT, ILM, UKSIS, KIXOB, and numbers like B411, G202, L223, W3) that connect the airspace over Bahrain with the airspace over Shiraz. This change is part of a larger traffic plan and will be in effect from June 3, 2026, to August 3, 2026. It's essentially an update to the 'highways in the sky' for aircraft.

OIIX A1113/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in Iran's airspace. It announces temporary changes to the specific routes (like designated highways in the sky) that domestic flights must use when traveling between Shiraz and Ahwaz airports. These changes are effective from early June to early August 2026 and are part of a larger traffic management plan.

OIIX A1175/26.explanation: A radar system in Mashhad, Iran, is currently broken and will be unavailable until tomorrow morning. This means air traffic controllers might not be able to see planes on their radar screens in certain parts of the airspace, specifically in Air Traffic Control Sector 7. This could affect how flights are managed in that area, potentially leading to delays or changes in flight paths.

OIIX A1181/26.explanation: Starting from June 8, 2026, and lasting until June 29, 2026, all aircraft flying in the western part of the Tehran airspace must have a special type of tracking equipment. This equipment, called a Mode S transponder with ADS-B or ADS-B Out, helps air traffic controllers see and track planes more accurately.

OIIX A1183/26.explanation: This message confirms that the air traffic control hand-off points between Tehran's airspace and the airspaces of Turkey (Ankara), Armenia (Yerevan), and Azerbaijan (Nakhchivan and Baku) are available for international flights that are flying into or out of Tehran's region. Essentially, it means international flights can be properly transferred between these countries' air traffic controllers.

OIIX A1184/26.explanation: From June 8th to June 29th, 2026, specific hand-off points (RAGET and PAXAT) between Tehran and Baghdad air traffic control can only be used by planes flying into or out of Tehran's airports between 1:30 AM and 3:30 PM UTC. However, flights for the Haj pilgrimage are allowed to use these points at any time.

OIIX A1185/26.explanation: This NOTAM means that all previous rules for planes flying over Iran (specifically the OIIX region) are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes have been put in place for planes flying above 28,500 feet during what's called a 'recovery period.' For example, planes flying from Oman to Pakistan must use a specific path (DENDA R462 METBI), and planes flying the other way must use another specific path (METBI R462 DENDA). This indicates an unusual situation requiring changes to how air traffic flows.

OIIX A1186/26.explanation: This notice means that for a specific period, all previous rules for planes flying high above 28,500 feet over Tehran's airspace (Iran) are temporarily stopped. New, specific flight paths have been set up for these high-altitude flights, especially for planes traveling between Karachi (Pakistan) and Baku (Azerbaijan) airspaces. This change is happening during a 'recovery period,' suggesting it's a response to a previous event or disruption.

OIIX A1187/26.explanation: This is an alert from Iran's air traffic control. It means that the usual rules for planes flying through Iran's airspace (specifically above 28,500 feet) are temporarily changed. All old rules for these flights are stopped. New, specific routes are now mandatory for planes flying between Pakistan and India (Kolkata) through this airspace. This change is in effect during what they call a 'recovery period,' suggesting there was a previous issue that required these new flight paths to be put in place.

OIIX A1188/26.explanation: This notice means that the usual rules for planes flying over a certain area (specifically above 28,500 feet) are temporarily stopped. New, specific flight paths are being put in place for planes traveling between two air traffic control regions (OAKX and UTAA) for a few weeks, during what's called a 'recovery period.' This means pilots must follow these new, designated routes instead of the old ones.

OIIX A1189/26.explanation: This aviation alert from Iran (Tehran airspace) means that all previous rules for planes flying over the country are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes must now be used for flights above 28,500 feet, especially for planes traveling between Afghanistan (Kabul area) and Azerbaijan (Baku area). This change is happening during what's called a 'recovery period,' suggesting there might have been a recent disruption or event.

OIIX A1190/26.explanation: Iran has temporarily changed the rules for planes flying over its airspace above 28,500 feet. All previous rules for these flights are stopped. New, specific routes have been created for flights traveling between Oman and Azerbaijan, and also for flights landing in the UAE, during a special 'recovery period'.

OIIX A1191/26.explanation: This message from Iran's air traffic control (Tehran FIR) means that all previous rules for planes flying over Iran at high altitudes (above 28,500 feet) are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes are now mandatory for these high-altitude flights, especially for planes traveling between Oman and Tajikistan, and vice-versa. This change is in effect during a 'recovery period,' suggesting there was a recent significant event or disruption.

OIIX A1192/26.explanation: All previous rules for planes flying over a specific region are temporarily suspended. New, specific flight paths must now be used for planes flying above 28,500 feet during a period of recovery. The NOTAM details these new routes for different directions and destinations.

OIIX B0290/26.explanation: For a specific period in June 2026, most private and visual flights (General Aviation and VFR flights) are temporarily stopped in Iran's airspace. However, there are exceptions for essential flights like training at certain airports, emergency services (hospital, search and rescue), government, and military operations. Some instrument flights that need to switch to visual rules for landing or takeoff are also exempt. Other visual flights can operate if they get special permission from the Civil Aviation Authority.

OIIX A1209/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots and aviation personnel. It announces that a new aircraft operating agency, similar to an airline, has been established. Its name is SOROUSH, and it will be identified by the three-letter code 'SHR' and the radio callsign 'SOROUSH'. This change is effective from June 10, 2026, to June 24, 2026, and applies to the Tehran flight information region.

OIIX A0982/26.explanation: Aviation authorities in Iran are letting pilots know that there will be no scheduled updates to their official aviation documents (like charts and procedures) on June 11, 2026, even though that was a planned date for updates.

OIIX A1208/26.explanation: A specific danger area in Iranian airspace, identified as OID127, will be active at certain times between June 13 and July 10, 2026. During these periods, from the ground up to 12,000 feet above sea level, aircraft should avoid this area, likely due to military exercises or other hazardous activities.

OIIX A1247/26.explanation: This is a routine administrative notice for the Tehran Flight Information Region (Iran). It provides a checklist of all currently active aviation notices (NOTAMs) and lists the latest updates to official aviation publications like AIP Amendments, Supplements, and Circulars. It also informs about specific dates when no new Airac AIP Amendments will be published. This NOTAM helps pilots and operators ensure they have the most current information.

OIIX B0292/26.explanation: This is an administrative notice for pilots and aviation personnel in the Tehran Flight Information Region. It acts like a checklist, telling them which other important aviation notices (NOTAMs) are currently active for the years 2022, 2025, and 2026. It also lists the latest updates to the official aviation manual (AIP), special supplements, and information circulars. Finally, it informs them that no new major updates to the AIP will be published on specific dates in June, July, and August 2026, and includes a note about a specific type of valid NOTAM. It's essentially a guide to help keep aviation documents and information up-to-date.

OIIX A1236/26.explanation: This is an alert for pilots about military gun firing exercises taking place in a specific area in Iran. The exercises will occur daily from June 16 to June 25, 2026, between 02:30 AM and 03:30 PM Universal Coordinated Time (UTC). The affected airspace extends from the ground up to 40,000 feet. Pilots flying in this region should be aware of this activity.

OIIX A1260/26.explanation: There will be gun firing in a specific area in Iran, near coordinates 30 degrees 20 minutes North, 49 degrees 50 minutes East. This activity will take place daily between 01:30 AM and 04:30 AM local time, starting from June 16th, 2026, and ending on June 25th, 2026. The gun firing will affect airspace from the ground up to 40,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX A1261/26.explanation: Pilots flying over Iran on specific routes (G665, L570, P715) must fly higher than usual. From June 16 to June 25, 2026, every day between 1:30 AM and 4:30 AM UTC, the lowest safe altitude on these routes has been raised to 42,000 feet. This means aircraft need to be at or above 42,000 feet when passing through these areas during those times for safety.

OIIX A1282/26.explanation: The airspace over Tehran, Iran, is mostly back to normal, but the western part is still closed to planes flying through. The eastern part is open, but planes flying there must follow special rules. These rules include contacting air traffic control at specific times, flying above 28,500 feet for radar coverage, having a working collision avoidance system, and maintaining specific transponder codes. Also, planes might be kept further apart than usual. These changes are in effect from June 17 to June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1284/26.explanation: This is an update about airports in the eastern part of Iran. Most airports in that area are open as usual, but some specific airports are completely closed, and others are only open during daylight hours. However, flights for the Haj pilgrimage can still use OIYY airport at night. All old flight approvals are no longer valid, and new ones must be obtained from the Iranian aviation authority.

OIIX A1285/26.explanation: This is an urgent aviation notice about airports in the western part of Tehran's flight information region. Most airports in this area are closed. However, a specific list of airports (OIAA, OISS, OICC, OIAW, OIFM, OISR, OIAM, OICI, and OISL) are allowed to operate, but only from sunrise to sunset. Additionally, special flights for the Hajj pilgrimage are permitted to operate at OIFM and OISS even during the night. All previously granted flight permissions are now cancelled, and airlines wishing to operate civil passenger flights must request new permission from the Iran Civil Aviation Authority.

OIIX A1237/26.explanation: Attention all pilots: There will be gun firing exercises taking place in a circular area with a 5-nautical-mile radius, centered near 26 degrees 15 minutes 38 seconds North latitude and 55 degrees 18 minutes 16 seconds East longitude. This activity will occur from the ground up to 15,000 feet above sea level. The exercises are scheduled for June 20th and 21st, 2026, specifically between 10:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC on both days.

OIIX A1238/26.explanation: This is an alert for pilots about a temporary change in flying rules. For two specific air routes (T800 and W146) between certain points (MIRIT, NANPA, and VELIX), the lowest altitude planes are allowed to fly has been increased to 17,000 feet. This change will be in effect from June 20th to June 21st, 2026, daily between 10:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC.

OIIX A1239/26.explanation: This is a warning for pilots about gun firing exercises. The firing will happen in a circular area, 5 nautical miles (about 9 kilometers) wide, centered near 25 degrees 52 minutes North, 55 degrees 02 minutes East (which is in the vicinity of Dubai/UAE). These exercises will take place from June 20th to June 21st, 2026, specifically between 10:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC each day. The firing will occur from the ground up to 15,000 feet in the air. Pilots should be aware of this dangerous activity in the specified area during these times.

OIIX A1240/26.explanation: There will be gun firing exercises in a specific area over the Persian Gulf (near 26°30'N 054°47'E) from June 20th to June 21st, 2026. The firing will happen daily between 10:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC. This activity will affect airspace from the ground up to 15,000 feet within a 5-nautical-mile radius of the specified location. Pilots should be aware of this danger.

OIIX A1241/26.explanation: For a specific period each day, the lowest safe altitude for planes flying on several designated air routes in the Tehran area has been increased to 17,000 feet. This means aircraft must fly higher than usual on these particular routes during those times for safety reasons.

OIIX A1242/26.explanation: This is an aviation alert for specific air routes (W152, M561, P574, T800/N717) in the Tehran region. From June 20 to June 21, 2026, during the hours of 10:30 AM to 2:30 PM UTC each day, the lowest safe altitude for aircraft flying on these routes has been increased to 17,000 feet. This means planes must fly at or above 17,000 feet when using these routes during the specified times.

OIIX A1248/26.explanation: Pilots need to be aware that there will be gun firing exercises in a specific circular area, about 5 miles wide, centered near the coordinates 26°14' North, 055°09' East. These exercises will occur daily between 10:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC, starting on June 20, 2026, and concluding on June 21, 2026. The affected airspace ranges from the ground up to 15,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX A1249/26.explanation: Attention pilots: There will be gun firing taking place in a specific area. This activity will occur on June 20th and 21st, 2026, daily between 10:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC. The affected zone is a 5-nautical-mile radius circle centered near 25 degrees 54 minutes North, 54 degrees 31 minutes East, from the ground up to 15,000 feet, with a small segment excluded. Aircraft should be aware of this danger during these times.

OIIX A1250/26.explanation: For a specific period on June 20th and 21st, 2026, between 10:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC each day, the lowest safe altitude for aircraft flying on certain air routes (T800, W146, and W152) in the Tehran region has been increased to 17,000 feet. This means planes must fly at or above 17,000 feet in these areas during those times.

OIIX A1251/26.explanation: This is a safety alert for pilots flying in the Tehran region. For a short period on June 20th and 21st, specifically between 10:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC each day, aircraft using Airway B547 between two navigation points (SIR DVOR/DME and IBNUX) must fly at or above 17,000 feet. This is a higher minimum altitude than usual, likely due to a safety concern like an obstacle or terrain.

OIIX B0294/26.explanation: From June 21st to June 28th, 2026, specifically on the 21st and 28th between 04:30 AM and 07:30 PM UTC, there will be live gun firing in a specific area near Tehran, Iran. This activity will affect airspace from the ground up to 8,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this danger.

OIIX A1202/26.explanation: This is a notice for pilots and aviation personnel. It means that the usual scheduled update to aviation charts and procedures, which normally happens on July 9, 2026, will not be published. There will be no new changes to aviation information on that specific date.

OIIX A1203/26.explanation: This is a notice for pilots and aviation personnel stating that there will be no new updates or changes to aviation charts and procedures published on August 6, 2026, as would normally be expected. Essentially, there's nothing new to report on that specific update cycle.

KICZ A0016/20.explanation: The U.S. government is warning American airlines, pilots, and aircraft operators to be very careful when flying over the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. This is due to ongoing military activity and political tensions in the region. They should review security information, avoid routes close to Iranian airspace to prevent being mistaken for something else by air defense systems, and be prepared for potential GPS and communication jamming. They must also report any safety or security incidents. A separate, stricter warning that bans U.S. flights over Iranian airspace is still in effect.

KICZ A0011/24.explanation: The United States government has issued a new rule (SFAR 117) that bans certain US flights from flying in the airspace over Tehran, Iran. This ban started on October 3, 2024, and is expected to last until October 31, 2027. All pilots and airlines are required to read the full details of this rule.
